content.text: embeds AWS compute and storage services directly inside telecom carriers' 5G networks. A Wavelength Zone is a physical compute environment co-located in the carrier's own data center — the same facility that houses the carrier's 5G User Plane Function (UPF). This means traffic from a 5G device reaches AWS compute without ever leaving the carrier's network, achieving round-trip latencies of
versus the 20–80 ms typical for a request traveling from a 5G device across the public internet to an AWS Region.

attrs.alt: AWS Wavelength Zone architecture: 5G device → carrier radio network → Wavelength Zone (EC2, EBS, VPC, carrier gateway) → AWS backbone → parent Region

content.text: 5G radios deliver sub-1 ms air-interface latency between a device and the nearest cell tower. The bottleneck is what comes next: the packet leaves the carrier network, traverses the public internet, reaches an AWS Region, is processed, and the response travels back — adding 20–80 ms for a typical metropolitan path. For most applications this is fine. For
applications — live game streaming at 60 fps, augmented reality overlays tied to physical location, autonomous vehicle sensor fusion, industrial robot control — even 20 ms is too much.

Wavelength's value proposition: keep the entire request → compute → response path inside the carrier's infrastructure. A 5G device in Boston talks to a Wavelength Zone in Boston's carrier data center — the packet never hits the public internet.
: sub-5 ms server response time eliminates perceptible lag for competitive players on 5G handsets.
: AR overlay computation (object recognition, spatial mapping) must complete before the user moves; > 10 ms introduces visible drift.
: sensor data (LiDAR, camera) streamed to edge compute for V2X (vehicle-to-everything) processing requires single-digit ms for safe reaction.
: real-time video mixing and transcoding from 5G-connected field cameras for broadcast workflows.
: PLC (programmable logic controller) feedback loops in factories where a network hiccup could halt production.
Wavelength Zone IDs follow the pattern
. Each zone belongs to one telecom carrier in one city. Multiple carriers in the same city have separate Wavelength Zones.

# List all Wavelength Zones and their status
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --filters Name=zone-type,Values=wavelength-zone \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].{ZoneName:ZoneName,State:State,Parent:ParentZoneName}' \
  --output table

# Opt in to a Wavelength Zone (required before use)
aws ec2 modify-availability-zone-group \
  --group-name us-east-1-wl1-bos-wlz-1 \
  --opt-in-status opted-in \
  --region us-east-1
The Carrier Gateway: Traffic Entry Point

is the networking component that connects 5G device traffic to a Wavelength Zone VPC subnet. It is the Wavelength equivalent of an Internet Gateway — but instead of routing traffic from the public internet, it routes traffic from the carrier's 5G network.
One carrier gateway per VPC per Wavelength Zone
: attach a single carrier gateway to your VPC, then associate it with the Wavelength Zone subnet's route table.

: the carrier gateway does NOT connect to the public internet. Traffic from a Wavelength Zone instance that needs to reach the internet must go through the parent Region (via the AWS backbone) and out through a Region Internet Gateway or NAT Gateway.
No inbound traffic from the internet
: only traffic originating from 5G devices on the carrier's network can reach Wavelength Zone instances directly. This is both a security property and a design constraint.
: instances in a Wavelength Zone can be assigned a carrier IP from the carrier's address space (not an AWS Elastic IP). This IP is routable from 5G devices on that carrier's network.

# Create a carrier gateway for your VPC in a Wavelength Zone
aws ec2 create-carrier-gateway \
  --vpc-id vpc-0abc123def456789 \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=carrier-gateway,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=bos-cagw}]'

# Get the carrier gateway ID
CAGW_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-carrier-gateways \
  --filters Name=vpc-id,Values=vpc-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'CarrierGateways[0].CarrierGatewayId' --output text)

# Create a route table for the Wavelength Zone subnet
WZ_RTB=$(aws ec2 create-route-table \
  --vpc-id vpc-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'RouteTable.RouteTableId' --output text)

# Add a default route through the carrier gateway
aws ec2 create-route \
  --route-table-id $WZ_RTB \
  --destination-cidr-block 0.0.0.0/0 \
  --carrier-gateway-id $CAGW_ID
Creating a Subnet and Launching EC2 in a Wavelength Zone

# Create a subnet in the Boston Wavelength Zone
WZ_SUBNET=$(aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id vpc-0abc123def456789 \
  --cidr-block 10.0.200.0/24 \
  --availability-zone us-east-1-wl1-bos-wlz-1 \
  --query 'Subnet.SubnetId' --output text \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=subnet,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=bos-wlz-subnet}]')

# Associate the Wavelength Zone route table with the subnet
aws ec2 associate-route-table --subnet-id $WZ_SUBNET --route-table-id $WZ_RTB

# Launch EC2 in the Wavelength Zone
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 \
  --instance-type t3.medium \
  --subnet-id $WZ_SUBNET \
  --security-group-ids sg-0abc123 \
  --placement AvailabilityZone=us-east-1-wl1-bos-wlz-1 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=bos-wlz-server}]'

# Assign a carrier IP address (required for 5G devices to reach the instance directly)
# First allocate a carrier IP
ALLOC_ID=$(aws ec2 allocate-address \
  --domain vpc \
  --network-border-group us-east-1-wl1-bos-wlz-1 \
  --query 'AllocationId' --output text)

# Associate the carrier IP with the instance's network interface
aws ec2 associate-address \
  --instance-id i-0bos123 \
  --allocation-id $ALLOC_ID
Wavelength Zones offer a very small subset of AWS services. The focus is on compute and local networking only — stateful services like RDS, managed databases, and even ElastiCache are not available.
VPC subnets, carrier gateway, carrier IPs, ALB, NLB
None natively — use Region via backbone
ECS (EC2 launch type), EKS worker nodes

Wavelength Zones have the smallest service footprint of all AWS specialized infrastructure. When your workload needs managed databases or serverless compute, it must call back to the parent Region over the backbone — which adds ~10 ms but is still far faster than reaching the Region directly from a 5G device.
Wavelength Zones vs Local Zones vs Outposts
AWS or partner co-lo in metro
EC2, EBS, ELB, RDS, ElastiCache, ECS

A Wavelength Zone subnet is an extension of the same VPC as the parent Region. EC2 instances in the Wavelength Zone share the same VPC CIDR, security groups, and IAM — they are just in a different AZ (the Wavelength Zone AZ).

# Full VPC setup: Region AZ + Wavelength Zone subnet + carrier gateway

# 1. Create VPC
VPC_ID=$(aws ec2 create-vpc \
  --cidr-block 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --query 'Vpc.VpcId' --output text)

# 2. Create Region AZ subnet (for control plane, databases, S3 access)
REGION_SUBNET=$(aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --cidr-block 10.0.0.0/24 \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a \
  --query 'Subnet.SubnetId' --output text)

# 3. Create Internet Gateway for Region subnet
IGW_ID=$(aws ec2 create-internet-gateway --query 'InternetGateway.InternetGatewayId' --output text)
aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway --internet-gateway-id $IGW_ID --vpc-id $VPC_ID

# 4. Create Wavelength Zone subnet
WZ_SUBNET=$(aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --cidr-block 10.0.200.0/24 \
  --availability-zone us-east-1-wl1-bos-wlz-1 \
  --query 'Subnet.SubnetId' --output text)

# 5. Create carrier gateway and route for WZ subnet
CAGW=$(aws ec2 create-carrier-gateway --vpc-id $VPC_ID --query 'CarrierGateway.CarrierGatewayId' --output text)
WZ_RTB=$(aws ec2 create-route-table --vpc-id $VPC_ID --query 'RouteTable.RouteTableId' --output text)
aws ec2 create-route --route-table-id $WZ_RTB --destination-cidr-block 0.0.0.0/0 --carrier-gateway-id $CAGW
aws ec2 associate-route-table --route-table-id $WZ_RTB --subnet-id $WZ_SUBNET

echo 'VPC, Region subnet, and Wavelength Zone subnet configured.'

Security groups work identically in Wavelength Zones as in Region AZs — they are attached to EC2 network interfaces and evaluated per packet. However, the source of inbound traffic is different: instead of internet IPs, inbound traffic comes from carrier IPs assigned to 5G devices.

# Security group for a Wavelength Zone game server
# Allow UDP game traffic from 5G devices (any carrier IP)
aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name wlz-game-sg \
  --description 'Wavelength Zone game server' \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID

SG_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-security-groups \
  --filters Name=group-name,Values=wlz-game-sg \
  --query 'SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' --output text)

# Allow UDP 7777 for game traffic from carrier network (0.0.0.0/0 via carrier gateway)
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $SG_ID \
  --protocol udp \
  --port 7777 \
  --cidr 0.0.0.0/0

# Allow SSH from Region CIDR only (for management)
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $SG_ID \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 22 \
  --cidr 10.0.0.0/24
Reaching the Internet from a Wavelength Zone Instance

Wavelength Zone instances cannot access the public internet directly through the carrier gateway. Internet-bound traffic must exit through the parent Region. Route traffic to the Region via the VPC's internal routing, then out through a Region NAT Gateway or Internet Gateway.

# Route internet-bound traffic from WZ instances through a Region NAT Gateway

# Create NAT Gateway in the Region subnet
REGION_EIP=$(aws ec2 allocate-address --domain vpc --query 'AllocationId' --output text)
NAT_GW=$(aws ec2 create-nat-gateway \
  --subnet-id $REGION_SUBNET \
  --allocation-id $REGION_EIP \
  --query 'NatGateway.NatGatewayId' --output text)
aws ec2 wait nat-gateway-available --nat-gateway-ids $NAT_GW

# Add a route in the WZ route table: non-local traffic → NAT GW via backbone
# (Traffic: WZ instance → Region NAT GW → internet)
aws ec2 create-route \
  --route-table-id $WZ_RTB \
  --destination-cidr-block 8.8.8.8/32 \
  --nat-gateway-id $NAT_GW

# More commonly, keep a specific CIDR for internet and default through carrier GW
# Carrier GW handles 0.0.0.0/0 for 5G-originated traffic; add specific routes for internet

: Each Wavelength Zone is one AZ in the carrier's data center. No redundancy within the carrier location. For HA, deploy a Wavelength Zone server AND a Region backup — use Route 53 health-check failover.
: A Wavelength Zone is bound to a single carrier. A T-Mobile device cannot reach a Verizon Wavelength Zone without leaving the carrier network.
: Only a handful of instance families are supported; GPU instances (g4dn) are available in some zones for rendering workloads.
: EC2 in Wavelength Zones is priced at 10–30% above equivalent Region instances. Carrier IP addresses have a separate per-hour charge.
: ALBs in a Wavelength Zone can only balance within that zone — no cross-zone load balancing to Region AZs from within the WZ.
: Container images are stored in ECR in the parent Region. ECS/EKS node startup in a WZ pulls images over the backbone — a cold-start cost (~100–500 ms) to account for in latency budgets.
Use Case Deep Dive: Real-Time Multiplayer Gaming
Real-time competitive games require server-authoritative state updates at 60–120 Hz — a server tick every 8–16 ms. For this to feel responsive, the
from the game client to the server must be under 10 ms. A 5G device connected to a Wavelength Zone game server in the same city can achieve RTTs of 2–6 ms.

# Game server architecture in a Wavelength Zone

# 1. Deploy a UDP game server on EC2 in the WZ
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0game12345 \
  --instance-type r5.xlarge \
  --subnet-id $WZ_SUBNET \
  --placement AvailabilityZone=us-east-1-wl1-bos-wlz-1 \
  --user-data '#!/bin/bash
  # Tune kernel for low-latency UDP
  sysctl -w net.core.rmem_max=26214400
  sysctl -w net.core.wmem_max=26214400
  sysctl -w net.core.netdev_max_backlog=5000
  # Start game server on UDP 7777
  /opt/gameserver/start.sh --port 7777 --tick-rate 128'

# 2. Assign carrier IP so 5G devices can connect
GAME_IP=$(aws ec2 allocate-address \
  --domain vpc \
  --network-border-group us-east-1-wl1-bos-wlz-1 \
  --query 'PublicIp' --output text)

# 3. Measure RTT from a 5G device (or simulate from a Region EC2)
# ping $GAME_IP  # should show < 5 ms from a 5G device in Boston
High Availability: Wavelength Zone + Region Fallback

A Wavelength Zone is a single point of failure at the carrier level. A carrier outage, power failure, or hardware failure in the carrier data center takes down the WZ. The standard HA pattern pairs the WZ deployment with a Region backup using Route 53 health-check failover.

CloudWatch agents on Wavelength Zone EC2 instances forward metrics to the parent Region CloudWatch endpoint over the backbone. Monitoring follows the same pattern as any EC2 instance — the AZ dimension will show the WZ identifier.

# Install and start CloudWatch agent
sudo yum install -y amazon-cloudwatch-agent
sudo /opt/aws/amazon-cloudwatch-agent/bin/amazon-cloudwatch-agent-config-wizard
sudo systemctl enable --now amazon-cloudwatch-agent

# Query RTT latency metric from a custom game server metric
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace GameServer/5G \
  --metric-name PlayerRTTms \
  --dimensions Name=AvailabilityZone,Value=us-east-1-wl1-bos-wlz-1 \
  --period 60 --statistics Average,p95,p99 \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '1h ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --end-time $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

# Publish custom RTT metric from the game server
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace GameServer/5G \
  --metric-name PlayerRTTms \
  --dimensions Name=AvailabilityZone,Value=us-east-1-wl1-bos-wlz-1 \
  --value 3.2 \
  --unit Milliseconds
t3.medium ~$0.046/hr in WZ vs ~$0.0416 in us-east-1
Different from Elastic IP pricing model
Traffic from carrier network not billed by AWS
No charge within same VPC across backbone
Carrier IPs are billed per hour even when not associated with an instance — release unneeded IPs immediately.
Savings Plans and Reserved Instances apply to Wavelength Zone EC2 just as in the parent Region — commit 1 or 3 years for up to 40% savings on sustained workloads.
Wavelength embeds AWS compute inside the carrier's 5G network
— traffic between a 5G device and the Wavelength Zone never leaves the carrier, achieving 1–5 ms latency.
(not an internet gateway) routes 5G device traffic into the Wavelength Zone VPC subnet — the only ingress path.
EC2, EBS, VPC, and ELB only
— no RDS, DynamoDB, Lambda, or S3. Stateful backend services live in the parent Region.
per account per zone, and each zone is associated with one carrier (Verizon, KDDI, SK Telecom, Vodafone, etc.).
Internet egress from a Wavelength Zone routes through the parent Region NAT Gateway — there is no direct internet path from the carrier gateway.
Compare before committing: Wavelength is the right choice only when your users are
and sub-5 ms latency is a product requirement — not just a nice-to-have.
